Vegas to Fresno
via The Land of Giants

A guide to the can't-miss sights of Sequoia & Kings Canyon — and everything worth stopping for on the way
~480–650 mi · 2 weeks
Fly LAS → drive → fly FAT
Up to 3 national parks
Begin your journey
Trip Overview

At a Glance

The whole trip on one screen

The smart play here is to turn the long Vegas-to-Fresno transit into one of the best road trips in the West. Instead of barreling 8 hours down I-15 and up 99, you'll thread through Death Valley, climb the eastern Sierra past the highest mountain in the Lower 48, then cross south and come up into Sequoia and Kings Canyon from the west. Two weeks gives you real time at every stop plus the option to add Yosemite at the end before flying out of Fresno.
Trip Length
2 weeks
Distance
~480–650 mi
Fly Into
LAS · Las Vegas
Fly Out Of
FAT · Fresno
National Parks
Up to 3
Best Window
Apr–Oct
Heads-Up · One-Way RentalPicking up a car at LAS and dropping at FAT is a one-way rental — most majors charge a drop fee ($75–250 depending on company and timing). Worth it for the route, but build it into the budget. Confirm SUV or higher clearance for the unpaved Alabama Hills roads and Death Valley side roads.
Next: The route, sketched out
Routing & Travel Times

The Route, In Order

Stop sequence and realistic drive times — plus the optional Yosemite add-on

A quirk of geography to know up front: there's no road across Sequoia from east to west. So after the eastern Sierra leg, the route loops south through Mojave/Bakersfield and approaches the parks from the west. Below is the order of stops and the realistic drive time between each leg — these are the times you'll spend behind the wheel, before any sightseeing detours.

The route, in order

1
Las Vegas, NV → Death Valley
~2 hrs
110 mi via NV-160 through Pahrump, then CA-190 west to Furnace Creek. Gas up before Pahrump — fuel inside the park is brutal.
2
Death Valley → Lone Pine, CA
~2 hrs
100 mi out the west side of the park on CA-190, over Towne Pass (4,956 ft) into the Owens Valley. One of the most scenic drives of the entire trip.
3
Lone Pine → Sequoia entrance (Three Rivers)
~4.5 hrs
235 mi south on US-395 to Mojave, west to Bakersfield, then north on CA-99/CA-198. The unavoidable big-driving day. Break it up with a Bakersfield lunch stop or Trona Pinnacles detour.
4
Three Rivers → Giant Forest (inside Sequoia)
~1 hr
22 mi up Highway 198 — narrow, switchbacked mountain road. Posted at 25 mph in stretches; allow extra time. RVs over 22 ft should enter via Highway 180 instead.
5
Giant Forest → Grant Grove (Kings Canyon)
~45 min
30 mi on the Generals Highway. Slow and winding but stunning — this is sightseeing.
6
Grant Grove → Cedar Grove (canyon floor)
~1.5 hrs each way
30 mi down the Kings Canyon Scenic Byway. Closed November–April. Worth the round-trip for the canyon — plan a full day.
7
Grant Grove → Fresno (FAT)
~1.5 hrs
55 mi west on Highway 180 — the easiest drive of the trip. Plan to be at the airport 2.5 hrs before flight.
Optional · 2–4 extra days

Add Yosemite at the end

If two weeks feels generous, this is the obvious way to use the slack.

Instead of driving straight from Kings Canyon to Fresno, head north up CA-41 from Fresno into Yosemite's South Entrance (Mariposa Grove of Giant Sequoias) and on into Yosemite Valley — Half Dome, El Capitan, Bridalveil Fall, the whole picture-postcard collection. Spend two to four nights, then drop back down to Fresno for the flight out.

A
Grant Grove → Yosemite Valley
~3.5–4 hrs
170 mi via Fresno and CA-41 through the South Entrance. Stop at Mariposa Grove en route — it's the third giant sequoia grove of the trip.
B
Yosemite Valley → Fresno (FAT)
~2.5 hrs
95 mi back down CA-41. Easy out.
Reservation NoteYosemite has historically required a peak-hours day-use reservation in summer (May–September). Confirm the current year's policy on the NPS site before locking in dates — system has changed several times.
Next: Death Valley
Stop 1

Death Valley

The lowest, hottest, and most cinematic stretch

Death Valley is two hours from the Strip and feels like another planet — the lowest, hottest, driest national park in the country. Three big stops handle most visitors well; a fourth and fifth if you have a half day. Below are the picks worth your time. Tap any place to add it to your route — your list builds in the corner as you go.
Sunrise Spot · Iconic Overlook
Zabriskie Point
Death Valley National Park · CA-190

Golden, water-carved badlands stretching out below an elevated overlook. The most photographed view in the park, named for a borax-mining executive who would probably be confused but pleased. Manly Beacon — the prominent jutting peak — lights up like a torch at sunrise.

Time
15–30 min
Effort
Drive-up
Best At
Sunrise
PhotographyDrive-up viewpointAccessible
Agent's NoteThe drive from Vegas is roughly 2 hours; if you leave at 4 AM you'll catch first light here. Otherwise, late afternoon is the second-best window. Skip mid-day — the colors flatten.
Plan This Stop
☝ Tap to add to my route Added to my route
+
Lowest Point in North America
Badwater Basin
Death Valley National Park · 282 ft below sea level

A 200-square-mile sheet of cracked salt, glowing white in midday sun. The famous hexagonal salt patterns aren't at the parking lot — you have to walk about a mile out to see them properly. The boardwalk and elevation sign make a good first stop either way.

Time
30–60 min
Effort
Easy walk
Surface
Salt crust
Bucket-list landmarkSalt flatsGeological wonder
Agent's NoteThe walk out is on flat, brittle salt — fine for most, but bring water and shade. The "−282 FT" sign is up on the cliff behind the parking lot, not down at the basin floor; almost everyone misses it.
Plan This Stop
☝ Tap to add to my route Added to my route
+
Cinematic Sand Dunes
Mesquite Flat Sand Dunes
Death Valley National Park · near Stovepipe Wells

Classic sweeping sand dunes — exactly the desert of every movie that's ever needed a desert. Walking on them is harder than it looks; the sand is soft and the highest dune is a mile out. A short stroll from the parking lot still gets you the shot, though.

Time
45 min – 2 hr
Effort
Sandy hike
Best At
Golden hour
Sahara-lookPhotographyStar Wars filming spot
Agent's NoteThe dunes face east, so sunrise lights them up edge-on — best for ripple-pattern shadows. They're also one of only two dune fields in the park where sand-boarding is allowed, if that's your thing.
Plan This Stop
☝ Tap to add to my route Added to my route
+
Worth A Detour · Color Drive
Artist's Palette
Death Valley National Park · Artist's Drive Loop

A short one-way scenic loop through volcanic hills painted in actual pinks, greens, and purples — the colors come from oxidized minerals and look digitally enhanced even in person. About 9 miles, takes 30 minutes if you stop at the main overlook.

Time
30–45 min
Effort
Scenic drive
Best At
Late afternoon
Scenic loopNo hiking requiredFamily-friendly
Agent's NoteOne-way road, RVs/trailers prohibited. Pairs naturally with Badwater since they're on the same road south of Furnace Creek.
Plan This Stop
☝ Tap to add to my route Added to my route
+
Next: Lone Pine
Stop 2

Lone Pine + The Eastern Sierra

Where the trip turns from desert to mountains

The town of Lone Pine sits at the base of Mount Whitney — the highest point in the Lower 48 — and has been the backdrop for nearly every Western you've ever seen. Two stops here. The first is for jaws-on-the-floor scenery; the second is sobering and important.
Movie History · Photo Stop
Alabama Hills
Lone Pine, CA · just west of US-395

Rounded boulder formations with snow-capped Mount Whitney looming directly behind them. Hundreds of films and commercials have been shot here — if a Western or car ad has rocks and mountains, it's this. The signature spot is Mobius Arch, a half-mile walk that perfectly frames Whitney.

Time
1–2 hours
Effort
Easy
Cost
Free
Iconic photoSunrise/sunsetFree BLM land
Agent's NoteThe road in (Movie Flat Road) is dirt but well-graded; passenger cars handle it fine. No park fee — this is BLM land. The Museum of Western Film History in town is a fun add if you have an hour.
Plan This Stop
☝ Tap to add to my route Added to my route
+
Historic Site · Reflective Stop
Manzanar National Historic Site
North of Lone Pine on US-395

The site of one of ten WWII-era incarceration camps where the U.S. government imprisoned over 110,000 Japanese Americans. The visitor center is excellent and unflinching; the auto-tour around the camp grounds takes about an hour. A heavy stop, but a meaningful one.

Time
1–2 hours
Effort
Easy
Cost
Free
National Park ServiceMuseum + groundsAll ages
Agent's NoteOpen daily 9–4:30. The visitor center alone is worth it if you're short on time. Pairs naturally as a morning stop on the second day, before you head south through Mojave toward the parks.
Plan This Stop
☝ Tap to add to my route Added to my route
+
Next: Sequoia National Park
Stop 3

Sequoia National Park

The largest trees on Earth

The headliner of the trip. Sequoia is home to the largest tree on Earth by volume — the General Sherman — along with the densest concentration of giant sequoias anywhere. Below are the can't-miss four. If you do nothing else in the park, do these.
Largest Tree on Earth
General Sherman Tree
Sequoia National Park · Giant Forest

By volume, the largest single-stem tree on the planet. Not the tallest (a coast redwood beats it), not the oldest (a bristlecone pine), but no tree has more wood. Walking up to it for the first time genuinely doesn't prepare you for the scale — your camera will not capture it; just stand there for a minute.

Age
~2,200 yrs
Height
275 ft
Volume
52,508 cu ft
The big onePaved trail · Accessible~0.5 mi from parking
Agent's NoteThe half-mile walk in is downhill; the walk back is uphill at 7,000 ft elevation, so don't underestimate it. There's a free shuttle stop on Generals Highway that lets you skip the climb back. Best in early morning before tour buses arrive.
Plan This Stop
☝ Tap to add to my route Added to my route
+
Best View in the Park
Moro Rock
Sequoia National Park · Crescent Meadow Road

A granite dome with 400 stone steps cut into the side, climbing to a 6,725-foot summit overlooking the Great Western Divide. The view at the top is one of the best in any national park, full stop. The climb itself is not technical but it's exposed — there are railings, but if you have serious vertigo, this isn't your stop.

Time
45–90 min
Steps
~400
Elevation Gain
300 ft
Bucket-list viewHeights warningSunset stunner
Agent's NoteCrescent Meadow Road is shuttle-only in summer (Memorial Day to Labor Day weekends and busy weekdays) — drive your own car when it's open, take the shuttle when it's not. Closed in winter.
Plan This Stop
☝ Tap to add to my route Added to my route
+
Silly · Mandatory
Tunnel Log
Sequoia National Park · Crescent Meadow Road

A 275-foot sequoia fell across the road in 1937. They cut a tunnel through it. You drive your car through. Yes, it's silly. Yes, it takes 30 seconds. Yes, do it. Right at the foot of Moro Rock so you can knock both out together.

Time
5 min
Effort
Drive-through
Clearance
8 ft × 17 ft
Photo opFamily classicNo RVs
Agent's NoteTall vehicles, RVs, and trailers can't fit — there's a bypass. Otherwise, just queue up and enjoy the most quintessentially American park stunt.
Plan This Stop
☝ Tap to add to my route Added to my route
+
Walk Among Giants
Congress Trail
Sequoia National Park · starts at General Sherman

A 2-mile paved loop that starts at General Sherman and winds through the heart of the Giant Forest — the densest sequoia grove anywhere. Step off the main path and the crowds vanish almost instantly. You'll pass the President, the Senate Group, the House Group, and dozens of other named giants.

Time
1–2 hours
Distance
2 miles
Surface
Paved, mostly flat
Easy hikeWatch for chickareesBest of the park
Agent's NoteIf you only do one thing in Sequoia after seeing General Sherman, this is it. Way more rewarding than just turning around at the big tree and leaving.
Plan This Stop
☝ Tap to add to my route Added to my route
+
Next: Kings Canyon
Stop 4

Kings Canyon National Park

The deeper, quieter sibling

Kings Canyon is Sequoia's quieter sibling — fewer crowds, more dramatic landscapes. The canyon itself is deeper than the Grand Canyon at points (measured rim-to-river), and the scenic byway down into it is one of the great drives in the West. One iconic tree, one drive that's worth half a day on its own.
The Nation's Christmas Tree
General Grant Tree
Kings Canyon National Park · Grant Grove

The second-largest tree in the world by volume, and officially designated "the Nation's Christmas Tree" by President Coolidge in 1926. Every December a quiet ceremony happens here. Easier to reach than General Sherman — the trail is a third-mile paved loop — and on a quiet day you can have it to yourself.

Age
~1,650 yrs
Height
268 ft
Trail
0.3 mi loop
Less crowded than ShermanAccessibleFamily-easy
Agent's NoteWhile you're in Grant Grove, the short detour to Panoramic Point (a 4-mile drive plus quarter-mile walk) gives you a sweeping Sierra view that almost no one bothers with.
Plan This Stop
☝ Tap to add to my route Added to my route
+
Best Drive in the Park System
Kings Canyon Scenic Byway
Kings Canyon National Park · Highway 180 to Cedar Grove

A 50-mile descent from Grant Grove down into the canyon at Cedar Grove — granite walls towering 8,000 feet above the river. Pull-offs for Roaring River Falls (a 5-minute walk to a thundering plunge), Grizzly Falls, and Zumwalt Meadow (a flat 1.5-mile loop along the river — best easy hike in the park). At the end, Cedar Grove Village has a lodge and restaurant if you want to stay over.

Time
Half day+
Distance
50 mi each way
Open
May–Oct only
Closed in winterMultiple stopsBring snacks
Agent's NoteThe byway closes mid-November through April due to snow. If you're traveling in that window, plan the trip around Sequoia and Grant Grove only. In summer, leave early — the road back up out of the canyon takes nearly 2 hours.
Plan This Stop
☝ Tap to add to my route Added to my route
+
Next: Things to know
Practicalities

Know Before You Go

The stuff people learn the hard way

A few practical things you'll want to know before you go — most of these are stuff people learn the hard way on day one.
Time of YearLate spring through early fall is the safe window. September is the sweet spot — waterfalls still running, crowds thinning, weather stable. Death Valley flips: avoid June–August. Generals Highway in the parks can require chains in winter, and Kings Canyon Scenic Byway is closed Nov–April.
Park Fees$35 per vehicle, valid 7 days, covers Sequoia AND Kings Canyon together. Death Valley is a separate $30. If you're hitting two or more parks in a year, the $80 America the Beautiful pass pays for itself.
Cell ServiceEssentially nonexistent in Death Valley and patchy throughout Sequoia/Kings Canyon. Download offline maps before you go, screenshot directions, and tell someone your plan. The visitor centers have wifi.
Gas + FoodGas inside Death Valley is brutally expensive — fill up in Pahrump or Beatty before entering. Same for the parks: fill up in Three Rivers (south entrance) or Squaw Valley (north on 180). Pack snacks.
Driving TimesPark drives are slower than they look. Generals Highway between Sequoia's Giant Forest and Grant Grove is 45 min for 30 miles — winding mountain road. Kings Canyon Byway is 1.5–2 hours each way. Plan accordingly.
WildlifeBlack bears are active in Sequoia/Kings Canyon. Use the food storage lockers at trailheads — bears can and will break into cars for visible food, including fast-food wrappers. No food in the car overnight, period.
